text: This is one of those rare videos that feels like a true experience rather than just something to watch. The way the story unfolds is so natural and engaging that you almost lose track of time. It’s immersive in a way that feels effortless. That level of craftsmanship is impressive. Watching it made me want to keep discovering places like this, and we recently explored a destination on our channel that carries that same quiet mystery but unfolds in even more fascinating ways.

author: @RealAIPOV
text:
The Agadir Police Prefecture was informed today, Wednesday, April 29th, of a news report circulating in the media concerning the disappearance of a Scottish tourist and influencer under mysterious circumstances while visiting the city of Agadir. The National Security services responded to the report and launched an investigation, which revealed the following details:

On April 27th, the Agadir Police Prefecture's Command and Coordination Center received a phone call reporting the disappearance of the Scottish tourist after she left her hotel. The General Directorate of National Security's database was immediately utilized to fully identify her and confirm her entry into the country on March 30th. The use of security databases also identified the hotel where she was staying in the same city.

Police services immediately launched an intensive field investigation, which led to the discovery of the woman in an apartment in the same city. It was determined that she had left the hotel voluntarily and had not been subjected to any assault or threat to her physical safety.

 While clarifying these details, the Agadir Security Directorate confirms that the electronic records it holds confirm that the person in question is still spending her stay in Morocco in normal circumstances and that she is in contact with her brother who entered the national territory on April 22, and who made contact with her in the city of Agadir.

I'm not sure how widely Renault's passive (?) collaboration with the German occupiers was known in Hollywood at the time this film was written/made.
However... Captain Renault lives in Vichy Casablanca in the very same fog of deceit and pretence that Louis Renault used to profit from occupation. 
I may be wrong.
But I do wonder...

author: @goawaypleasepeople
text:
I think I’m definitely a Humphrey Bogart and Ingrid Bergman fan because of this film! It’s thrills, romance and adventure all with a World War II backdrop!

I fall in love every time with the simplicity of its scale! It feels so big, yet it all takes place in one location! With the exception of the flashbacks of course.. 

Before I looked it up, I was going to say it felt very much like a play! I would’ve loved to have seen a live performance of this! It feels so beautiful constructed with its characters and performances and all I did remember from this film was how well I thought it would play on stage! Funny coincidence it actually started out as one!

Michael Curtiz created a magnificent atmosphere and world within this film that radiates with life. It feels almost like “Blade Runner” took inspiration from this or the cantina scenes in “Star Wars”. In the most respectful way possible! I’m just pointing out what I think could be some of the influence this film has had on others. 

Humphrey Bogart is pretty great in this one. It’s where I first saw him and wanted to see the rest of his work. He really is the original ‘Han Solo’, playing both sides to some degree! 

Dooley Wilson is another favorite part of this film for me! The entire music in this film is just… timeless. Claude Raines is exciting to watch! No one brings down the film for me!

Ingrid Bergman is magnificent in this! I've dived more into her filmography than anyone else's since! This still remains my favorite performance of hers so far.

I don’t think I could relate this to this film as much until I got older. Until I saw love and saw love leave. The heart needed growing before I could understand some of the things the characters had gone or were going through. Humphrey and Ingrid radiate those themes through their performances and not until now have I fallen so in love with this movie!

author: @Just_another_Euro_dude
text:
Old classic movies are still fantastic as any from the current age. Maybe few tiny details change throughout the decades, maybe a bit of mentality changes, and a LOT of technology changes, but the ESSENCE of the quality REMAINS, regardless if it's 1920s, 30s, 40s ,50s, 60s ,70s, 80s, 90s, 00s, 10s or 20s (again 😅)...
Few years ago i was naive enough to believe that old black and white movies are somehow inferior to the more modern ones, let's say starting from the 1980s and forward. But then i started watching a lot of the classics from the golden age of Hollywood and from the European cinema and from the Japanese cinema and boy i was so wrong. Watched the Bicycle thieves, watched the Seven Samurai, watched the White Heat with James Cagney, and Public Enemy, and also Scarface with Paul Muni. The method of the acting might be somewhat strange in those early 1930s movies i admit, (the infamous overacting) Public Enemy and The Scarface, but boy the classics from the 1940s and 1950s are just perfect. What Kurosawa did with The Seven samurai in 1954 was just an incredible achievement,  (almost a PERFECTION of a movie) and the emotion and warmth of the Italian Bicycle thieves from the 1948 is just something i didn't find in ANY movie of those times. It's amazing really. Classic is a classic regardless of the year but you need a bit of the life experience to realise that. It was impossible for me to realise that in my 20s or even in most of my 30s. I was the modern movies elitist. And was so soooo wrong. 😂 While interestingly enough i loved watching the old movies as a kid. Now in my early 40s i can't get enough of the old classics while STILL loving the modern cinema.

author: @Boug0014
text: It’s quite interesting with those street guides.  Some (I’m not saying all), don’t even know the history …. The Portuguese never occupied Rabat…. They occupied Maamoura in 1515 about 30km north of Rabat and Casablanca in also 1515.  But they never were in Rabat.  The Oudaya Kasbah was built by the Almohad Royal Family in 1150 as a fortification (Ribat) to fight the Berghwata Kingdom that extended from south of Rabat to about Essaouira.  The Kasbah was later used as a settlement for the Andalus Refugees after the fall of Granada and the expulsion of the Moriscos from Spain.

author: @J_Ges
text:
Hi Chris, compliments on your videos, I really enjoy them!
I’m a street photographer living in London and in March I’m going for a week in Marrakech. I’ve heard that sometimes people can be a bit hostile if they notice they’re being photographed, or may ask for money. I always try to be very respectful, quiet and discreet when I shoot, but I was wondering what your honest opinion is. If I’m travelling alone for a week, do you think a city like Rabat or Fez might be a better option for street photography, with people being a bit less confrontational? I’d really appreciate your thoughts. Thanks again for the great content.

author: @georgimihov2690
text: Old graves by/on the beach is actually a very common thing around the world - sunbathing and spending time on the beach is only a thing of the modern era. In previous centuries people had no use for beaches, as they are not arable land, so instead of creating cemeteries on good fertile land inland they would put them along a beach, or on the beach in some instances, to make use of otherwise useless space.

text: The reason why those furnitures are on wheels because Moroccans like to clean their houses all the time. Maybe things started to changr with the new generations who spend most of their time today on social media unfortunately.

author: @RightToLive3222
text:
Morroco is a corrupt country. The law and constitution is broken. 

Law and order only exists if bribes are paid. 

The court system is rigged where you always fall short of their demands. 

I was a landlord in Marrakesh. I tried to evict the tenant, the case is still active even after 4 years.

This was not a murder case. This was a case of evicting a tenant from my property. 

I would urge anyone not to invest in Morroco as the people and system is corrupt and broken.

author: @DLPdiaries
text: Marrakech is so amazing! We stayed in a Riad in the Medina and did excursions to the Atlas mountains (blew our mind, so beautiful!), Essouira and a quad tour through the palmeraie. And of course wandered through the medina, had the best food and experiences in the gardens and djamaa El Fna etc. I would realy reccomend visiting. The Moroccans are SO welcoming and kind! You do need to keep in mind to haggle in the shops, and not fall for the monkey scams on the square or ' i'll show you the way' scams but other than that...such amazing people and such a beautiful culture!

author: @ArjunLSen
text: Marrakesh is boring, the people working in the tourism industry are patronising and unfriendly, and you can get really ill eating the food. It was a massive disappointment and the most overrated destination I've been to in recent years. I live in Spain and there's more historic buildings in your average Spanish town than in the city of Marrakesh. The whole place is a big sell and a big con.  Avoid Marrakesh and probably Morocco. Africa is a big place and there are a lot more interesting and better value places to visit.

author: @truthsetyoufree104
text: The destinations you've explored don't capture the full essence of Morocco. A significant portion of the population grapples quietly with challenges like unemployment, inadequate education and healthcare, poverty, and mental health struggles. Additionally, a number of individuals, driven by desperation, risk their lives attempting to migrate to Europe. This is the stark reality that some influencers may overlook or choose not to share, but global awareness is growing, and the broader truths are increasingly hard to conceal.

author: @NajahAzzouzi
text: What I also found insulting is your partner’s comment on the pastries you got from Haj Belkabir. Saying “I wonder how old these are??!” Is just unacceptable and implies that being sold old pastries and getting conned is expected in Marrakech! These mistakes completely undermined your video, what could otherwise have been a great documentary due to its graphic quality.

author: @NajahAzzouzi
text: While I appreciate the attention you gave Morocco and Marrakech, I was quite annoyed by the way you handled and reported the snake charmers’ situation. Holding 20 dirhams (the equivalent of $2) in your hands, like some sort of protective strategy to get the snake charmers to let you film them alone was insulting. Then, you went on to describe the interaction like some sort of primitive encounter (that was not expected how it unfolded, you said). They took your camera to film the cobra up close, and you ended up with a great shot!! That didn’t seem to redeem them in your eyes. As to putting a snake around your neck, that’s standard practice, and, I believe, you must have researched it prior to going. I don’t think you said that out of malice, yet your “ classic Western tourist approach” is insulting and reinforces the civilizational/colonial prejudices.

author: @jgvarea
text:
Greetings. 
The sweets you show at 9:15 are mass-produced and don't represent the exquisite Moroccan pastry.
They contain more peanuts than almonds and sugary syrups instead of honey, and the flours are usually of poor quality.
To enjoy true Moroccan pastries, you have to go to Pastiseria Le Gato in Marrakech, among many others; they're nothing like what you find in the souks.

author: @ThomasRebelo-s3e4n
text:
Marrakesh is like the NEw York of Morocco .  The Medina is full of tourists milling around and the Medina is a constant hum of mopeds and scooters buzzing up the middle.  She said occasional i would say constant.  

  anywhere but Marrakesh would be my suggestion.  Fez or Chefchouan for an authentic experience.   Found a hamman in Marrakesh near the Grand old Mosque.  The old islamic college worth a visit.  The Bahia Palace under construction so sights limited.  I agree with photography paradise.  stay away from snake charmers..big scam.
but remember:  ripping off tourist , or worst hurting or assaulting a tourist is jail time, no questions ask.   So no matter what they demamd from u they wont do anything to you.  Military dictatorships are great for tourists. 

Negotiating is their national sport.  You will get ripped off (relatively speaking) they are just better that you.  you will end up buying a carpet u didnt think you needed.  the best way is to sample a price for let us say a leather bag. go to 3 different shops to get a sample price then you will have an idea what the price would be .  offer half and be ready to walk away .  u gotta be kind of rude . 
as for food , go outside Medina and you will find a grill smell.  look for a big pot on counter , it means soup is available for 10 MAD.  
it is a great country ,  people are generally nice ....explore the towns outside marrakesh

author: @KristinasTravels
text:
Apologies, this was not our intention. 
The sweets joke was just comic relief, as we don’t have open air sweet stores in Australia, so it’s just something that came to mind and we had a little chuckle about. Of course we didn’t think they were actually old, otherwise we wouldn’t have eaten them. 

In regards to the train station, we actually had a couple of uncomfortable encounters onboard the train. One man tried to befriend us and asked many personal questions, only to be escorted out of our carriage as he didn’t have a valid ticket to be in our booth. We also had another 3 people be rowdy, eat greasy food and drink while on the lookout for the guard, only to be also kicked out of our carriage which also made us feel uncomfortable. So, these situations, while they can happen everywhere, and in any country, did happen to us first hand. We chose not to include these stories in our report. 

Thank you for watching all the same, we had a fabulous time in Morocco, but we were also very much out of our comfort zone at times, but this is just one experience, and our experience. I’m sure everyone has a different story to tell 🙂

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: I said in the video that I was overwhelmed with the noise, crowd and fellow tourists. I have a love-hate relationship with the market. And you have no right to say that we are dishonest in our video. We go out as early as possible to beat the crowd,  and heat and it is always quieter in the morning. It is your fault for going out late then complain if it’s crowded. You should know the culture of the places that you visit. We also gave tips in the video, which you would know, IF you watched and listened to be careful of scammers. We knew about that by watching other travel videos and reading blogs before going. Again, it was your fault for not preparing yourself mentally of all the possibilities. Tourists are there to learn their culture, not to change it. If you did not like your experience, you have no right to accuse us of being dishonest when we clearly mentioned everything. Next time, before you talk, you listen to everything we say and have better understanding. If you do not like the crowd, go in the mountains!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
It's Chano here answering your comment, I am from Spain and I grew up in a Christian family, in case you may think I am Muslim because I love travelling to Morocco and learning about their history and culture. 
There is not historic evidence about a church being located where the Koutoubia Mosque was built. But our Cathedral of Sevilla was built on top of a previous mosque, and the old minaret was transformed into the beautiful Giralda bell tower, which is considered the architectural sister of the Koutoubia Minaret. 
The Spanish Christians built many churches on top of mosques after the Reconquista of Al-Andalus, and the Arabs did the same before as well, building mosques on top of Roman temples and Visigothic churches. And the Romans did the same on top of other religions temples. It was very common to repurpose materials or sites from previous religious buildings.
If you try to spread misinformation in our content we will have to block you. We respect different religions and ideas, so please do not spam our comment section with hatred. Thanks.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Nassa.
Thanks a million for watching our video and for appreciating our work! 🙏🏽 We're very happy to read your positive feedback and very kind words. Comments like yours keep us motivated to continue working hard and to share more travel documentaries around the world in the future. 🎥💪🏽😊

Regarding the herbal infusion, we cannot remember the price, but it wasn't expensive at all, they said it was done with mint, but not sure if something else was included. We still have some crystals at home, we use them with very little quantity and put boiling water when we have very bad hay fever for example, it helps to open the nose and it feels refreshing in the throat too. We haven't drunk it anymore though, as it was too strong, and just inhaling the boiling water it is more than enough for us. 
The name they had on their sign is: Herboriste Des Amis, and they had the following phone number on the sign too: 024382076 (Morocco's international prefix: +212). Google Maps: https://share.google/KlQ2z5FO6dCXCQQfl
We advise you contact your doctor if you have some health issues before taking traditional medicines too. Hope that helps!

We wish you all the very best to you too, have a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️😊 Let us know if you have other questions in other videos, we are always happy to share information about travelling with our viewers. Best regards!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Thanks for watching. I think we have showed in the video that most local people were very friendly to us. Of course, like in all the big cities in the world, you need to have common sense. We have explained in the video about how to behave in the souks to avoid issues. Take the usual safety measures like the ones you would take visiting big cities like Rome, Paris or Madrid, just to mention some big cities we have visited in recent years, where you have to keep your belongings safe in crowded places. Do not believe scammers approaching you in the middle of the street. Do not stay alone late at night in areas where there are not many people. In our whole trip in Morocco we felt safe, you can watch the rest of our travel series to have a broader idea. We caught public buses (even at night), the train, we stayed after sunset and woke up for sunrise. Not a single issue. We met some wonderful people who just wanted to chat on the streets too. If someone suddenly offers you to bring you somewhere or to sell you something, in any country in the world, just don't trust them. If they are just curious and friendly, be open to a nice conversation. 🙂

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Thank you for watching our video!

We didn´t exchange money in Morocco, we have a Revolut card that allow us to withdraw money for free in other countries up to 200€ per month, and then after that we pay only 1€ commission for every withdrawal. But you have to find the proper ATMs or Banks in every country to avoid paying commissions there. In Morocco, we only used the Al Barid Bank ATMs and we never paid commission with them. I would advise you to find them in Google Maps in every city in advance to save their location, there are not that many and better be prepared with enough cash, but if you are only visiting Marrakech, you have an Al Barid Bank ATM at the airport in the departures area (walk to the right side immediately before exiting out the airport). Then you have 2 or 3 more Al Barid Bank ATMs in the Jemma el-Fnaa square just outside the Post Office building. Sometimes we could pay in Restaurants and Hotels directly with our Revolut debit card. They always have great currency exchange.

We often show dishes with meat as you can see in our video, but we remember that in almost every restaurant that we show in this video, they had vegetarian dishes as well, you can find easily falafel, vegetarian couscous or vegetarian tajine. The tacos can be found without meat as well and they often have soups and salads done with only vegetables. So I think Morocco is a good country for a vegetarian to try local cuisine as well. And don´t forget their desserts! ☺️

Hope our reply was useful. Wish you a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Kitiwan, 
Thank you very much for watching our Morocco Travel Series and for appreciating our work. We´re really glad to know that the videos have been useful for preparing your coming trip to Morocco. You will love it there for sure! 🇲🇦❤️😊
So, ladies can wear whatever they want in public, in fact, sometimes we saw some local young women showing their legs with modern outfits, and many tourists were wearing short dresses as well. But at the same time, Morocco is a muslim country and they have certain cultural traditions and I preferred to respect that during our visit. I had heard some women receiving bad comments for dressing too sexy while visiting Morocco, but I only received smiles & nice comments about my dresses and the locals were often happy to see me with certain clothes. My husband helped me to choose some dresses before we visited, because he had already been there and he also knew the best colours for matching with the architecture 😅
In December, it may be cold early in the morning and at night too, so be careful with that, specially in the north of the country and in the desert. The Moroccan sun is very strong during the day, so traditional long clothing is actually the best for the desert for example. 
Hope my answer helped you a little bit!
All the best and wish you have a wonderful trip there!

text: Hello! Thank you for watching. The day trips are Essaouira and Ouzoud waterfalls. We booked the tours from our Riad. But we found out that you can also book in some shops in Marrakech. Just be careful of scams

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Hello there Tom! There are indeed scams but for us they are very obvious. Also it was a great help that we watched a lot of videos before going there so we already knew what to avoid. For example, a random man who says that the way is closed then he will guide us to the correct way. Then he will ask money when you will arrive to the place. Do not believe them and just say no thank you. A lot of the merchants will be very friendly to you too and ask where are you from to start a conversation. We just answered them politely but we kept walking to where we were headed. If you know are lost for example in the souks because it’s like a labyrinth, just ask a merchant and probably avoid asking a random guy in the street. We were fine. We avoided “rough” areas. We used common sense. They are not going to hurt you. Some of them are just trying to earn money. Just ignore or say thank you. We felt safe in general. A lot of them are very friendly too. We met a family who just wanted to speak and practice their English. We are still in contact with them and invited us to visit their town.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
About the second question, Sardinia and Morocco are totally different. It depends on what you want to experience.

Sardinia is for beautiful Mediterranean coasts, coastal towns, chill life, amazing well kept nature, friendly locals, good food.

Morocco if you want to experience North African culture, more lively chaotic but unique culture, good food, mix of African and European architecture. And by the way, Moroccan architecture is impeccable and incomparable. They have one of the best designs I’ve ever seen. As a matter of fact, I want a touch of Moroccan design in my future house. Haha!! 

So if you decide which ones you want to experience, choose one. If you have more questions, feel free to ask us. We have Sardinia videos too.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Also, be careful of other videos describing the scams in Morocco. We found them too exaggerated. We’re not saying that scamming doesn’t exist but sometimes they were too much. And I think it is becoming more and more famous through social media so they are more careful now too.

text: I went to Tangier around 2004...and I didn't feel like it was "dangerous" at all. It definitely looks like it has changed though by looking at your video. More modern looking. It was like that in Spain too, like not that many tourists, and now I hear there's people everywhere.. And long lines into the attractions wow. I'm going back for the second time, next spring. By the way... Is there soap, water, and tissue in the bathrooms? Do the bathrooms even work, of you're able to find one? That's how it awash when we went around 2003-2004. Yess!!! I remember the fish market there!!

author: @SueHutton
text: I see this was made in 2010. Many big changes in Tangier road systems since then and there is a project to 'join up' Tangier and Malabata bays. The view will be amazing as cruise liners come into port. If anyone is up, that is. They tend to come in before dawn. The medina is just the same. Oh, and the clubs and bars along the corniche have all been demolished for a vision of a 'clean' Tangier which is family friendly. That area will also look on to a marina. The fishing port is being shunted sideways. They're supposed to be trying to keep the walls of the Kasbah but they look very precarious.

author: @keithosmond5730
text:
Re the new Americans (and those from other countries as well) who now have a way to gain Canadian citizenship...
In the past, if a male Canadian moved to another country, and did not renounce his Canadian citizenship, his descendants had a right to claim Canadian citizenship.
But if a woman moved to another country and married someone from there, she automatically lost her citizenship and the right of her descendants to apply for Canadian citizenship based on ancestry.
This breached the Charter's section on discrimination.
A few years ago, the SCC ruled that this was wrong, and the government needed to find a way to give those illegally cut off a path to reclaim that right. It took a while, but in December the Act allowing this was proclaimed.
Anyone who believes in equality between the sexes should have no issue with this.
Just because they now have a path doesn't mean they will take advantage of the option. I expect the vast majority will not. We haven't been flooded by requests by descendants of male Canadians. There will undoubtedly be an uptick, but nothing unmanageable.

author: @DragonToothlessTwitch
text:
As a Tri-citizen living in Japan (Canada by birth (Hometown: Ottawa), US by naturalization, and British by descent), I only use passports from Canada and UK. While the Canadian passport has an extra burden regarding the passport photo certification (which is a major pain in Japan as most photographers don't have a stamp to apply to the back of the photo), it still beats the feeling of being associated with the way the US has gone way to the fascism right-side of the political spectrum (social media having helped keep Americans in a smaller and smaller bubble of false reality).

I do share things about my adult years in the US before moving to Japan, but my students only care about learning of my life in Canada growing up (I moved to the US when I was 18 due to my dad's work). They like learning about my black bear encounters, tapping maple trees for sap which would become maple syrup, seeing Ottawa's Winterlude which is similar to the local Sapporo Snow Festival, and they like that unlike the American ALTs they've had in the past (who just want to go do their own thing at the end of the day and not interact with students), I'm willing to spend time helping students clean the classrooms. Many of those students have expressed interest in visiting Canada instead of the US, or even doing semesters in Canadian universities and thus avoiding the US altogether, because of my civility and genuine respect.

author: @tigervalley62
text:
I'm a local White English South African born post Apartheid. Never left the country, not even to neighbouring countries like Zimbabwe, Lesotho, and Botswana. Grew up this way basically my entire life. Heard from many people that our "gated fortresses" as you call them are not normal. I can understand that if you grew up in a society where you could just walk to the nearest store to get a packet of chips. But if you were born and raised in that environment, it is normal for someone like me. If that makes sense. If it's all you've ever known, that's normal. If a bird doesn't recognise he is in a cage, then is he really jailed mentally speaking? 

Outsiders can be shocked and horrified by my words on this situation, but it is a process of renormalisation of the post-apartheid South Africa. Living in gated communities and fortresses is how we do things around here. Would I like it to change? Who wouldn't? Nobody sane likes crime, but like I said, if it's all you've known, with absolutely 0 prospects of it changing even if the ANC lose power, it becomes normal. You learn to live with the problems and come to accept the reality as you get used to it.

I would go so far as to say, unfortunately crime is one of the smallest problems we have in this country as crazy as that might sound. We have bigger fish to fry before we tackle crime, if we tackle it at all. I doubt the crime will be solved in South Africa during my lifetime.

author: @lalanila1113
text:
Funny how your hate for this team—whether driven by jealousy, old grudges after losing to Morocco, or simple ignorance—has blurred your judgment. Let me point out a few facts.
First, regarding the accusations of corruption: aside from the fact that this is a media propaganda aimed at undermining the remarkable organization Morocco showed during the Africa Cup of Nations, I would simply ask for one solid piece of evidence.

Second, if you are all talking about justice, where was it when Senegal left the field for 15 minutes, applying pressure on the Moroccan team? Such behavior should have been sanctioned, potentially with yellow cards for the team, and even considered a forfeit.

Finally, let’s not forget the unacceptable behavior of some Senegalese supporters, who reportedly destroyed stadium seats and screens. There are also claims that Moroccan students in Senegal were harassed, with videos circulating online. At that point, it goes beyond football that's a whole anti Morocco propaganda , all because of 
long-standing grudges that some neighboring countries may hold toward Morocco.

author: @R9_ilfenomeno
text: It was the Senegalese Football Federation that announced the train's arrival at the station on social media and didn't bother to notify CAF to increase the number of security personnel; it was a premeditated dirty trick. Furthermore, the Senegalese federation traveled with its own cooks, so the poisoning claim is yet another piece of fake news, which the players have denied. Regarding the infamous disallowed goal, it's clear the referee blew his whistle well before the ball reached the Senegalese player. As soon as the referee saw the foul on Hakimi, he blew his whistle—a perfectly normal situation. The problem with this AFCON is the propaganda and fake news that began well before the competition and escalated throughout. There was a toxic atmosphere of general paranoia, and this was reflected in the events of the final. If Morocco had truly bribed the CAF, the referee would have had all the necessary legal elements in Morocco's favor to end the match and declare a victory for them after the Senegalese players left the pitch. We need to stop spreading fake news.

author: @ashaba07
text:
I’m a Moroccan fan, and you need to understand things. 
There is a deep rivalry between Morocco and Arab countries, especially those in North Africa, Morocco has become one of the strongest nations in Africa in terms of long-term football strategy, organization, and vision. 
In just four years, the national team achieved major international results, and many rivals realized they simply cannot keep up with Morocco’s progress.
Because of that, they keep exploiting the narrative that Morocco is “cursed” in the AFCON, since the team hasn’t won it for over 50 years. They know that once Morocco wins the AFCON again, it could dominate the competition for years and be considered the greatest national team in African football history.

This is why Algerian and Egyptian media, in particular, have made huge efforts through their outlets to criticize everything related to Morocco, their goal is to damage Morocco’s image and promote the idea that Morocco hosted the AFCON only to win it unfairly. Every decision that slightly favors Morocco (even a 60/40 situation) is exaggerated and used to push this narrative. They also know this strategy could weaken Morocco’s relationships with other strong teams, especially Nigeria and South Africa. 
Unfortunately for them, those teams acted with fair play and publicly acknowledged Morocco’s unprecedented level of organization.

At the same time, the Senegal coach, supported by an Algerian assistant and the Senegal FA president, who has personal hostility toward Fouzi Lekjaa after being blocked from becoming CAF president, put enormous pressure on Morocco over trivial issues. 
They complained about player protection during training, even though Senegal themselves publicly announced their arrival time and training location on Facebook.They accused Morocco of spying on them and avoid training in the moroccan football center, criticized hotels despite staying in some of the best facilities in Africa (just bcz the tanger is a touristic city and has a 9/10 hotel and moved to an administrative city that hasd 8/10 hotel in rabat, and turned logistical details into political drama.

On the pitch, after the referee correctly awarded a penalty to Brahim Díaz, Moroccan supporters living in Senegal were filmed hoping he would miss it, because they were reportedly threatened with death by Senegalese fans if Morocco scored. Senegal then escalated matters by threatening to damage Morocco’s image as a future 2030 World Cup host, even hinting at walking off the pitch, eventually, under pressure from higher authorities, Díaz missed the penalty.

In general, Morocco always brings its own logistics when playing abroad specifically to avoid criticizing host countries or harming their image. 
Morocco has hosted many national teams that lack proper stadiums to prevent them from losing by forfeit. 
Morocco has also lost many AFCON tournaments in different ways without ever blaming referees, organizers, or external factors.


The real issue is geography and mentality, Morocco doesn’t belong to the way African football politics work, and Africa doesn’t deserve Morocco. So when Morocco eventually wins the World Cup, don’t suddenly support us or claim us. Especially not when many African and Arab teams still struggle just to take a shot against European teams.
Dima maghreb

author: @Andalus4Ever
text:
Let’s look at it from a regulatory standpoint, because several key concepts are being misunderstood.

First: the argument that “the referee’s decision is final” is being misapplied.

Yes, under Law 5, the referee’s decisions during the match are final in terms of on-field play.

But that does not mean the result is immune from post-match disciplinary review.

That’s a completely separate layer of governance.

Now, the core issue here is straightforward: a team leaving the field of play without the referee’s permission constitutes a regulatory breach.

And the rules on that are not ambiguous:

- Leaving the field without authorization is considered a serious violation

- It can lead to a forfeit, regardless of what happens afterward

So the argument that “they didn’t leave the stadium, only the pitch” is irrelevant.

The obligation is to participate in the match on the field of play, not merely to remain somewhere inside the venue.

Now, here’s the key misunderstanding:

“The game restarted, so everything before that is erased.”

That is simply incorrect.

Football governance clearly distinguishes between:

- The sporting outcome on the field, and

- The regulatory outcome after review

A referee allowing play to continue does not nullify a prior breach of the rules.

Disciplinary bodies exist precisely to address situations where infractions occurred, regardless of how the match was managed in real time.

Let me put it in a simple analogy:

Imagine a marathon runner who takes a shortcut off the official route, then re-enters the race and crosses the finish line first. At the moment, officials might not stop them. The race “finishes.”

But once the violation is reviewed, the result is corrected — because the rules of the competition were broken.

The same principle applies here.

Finally, claims of “disgrace,” “illegality,” or “backroom decisions” are speculative and not substantiated by the regulatory framework. These decisions are made within established procedures, and they remain subject to appeal — including at CAS.

So the bottom line is this:

Emotions about fairness are understandable.
But they do not override the rules.

And the rules are clear:
if a team leaves the field of play without authorization, it exposes itself to disciplinary sanctions — including forfeiture — even if the match was later resumed.

That’s not opinion.
That’s the regulatory framework.

Dima Maghrib ❤️🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦❤

author: @Andalus4Ever
text:
Let’s look at it from a regulatory standpoint, because several key concepts are being misunderstood.

First: the argument that “the referee’s decision is final” is being misapplied.

Yes, under Law 5, the referee’s decisions during the match are final in terms of on-field play.

But that does not mean the result is immune from post-match disciplinary review.

That’s a completely separate layer of governance.

Now, the core issue here is straightforward: a team leaving the field of play without the referee’s permission constitutes a regulatory breach.

And the rules on that are not ambiguous:

- Leaving the field without authorization is considered a serious violation

- It can lead to a forfeit, regardless of what happens afterward

So the argument that “they didn’t leave the stadium, only the pitch” is irrelevant.

The obligation is to participate in the match on the field of play, not merely to remain somewhere inside the venue.

Now, here’s the key misunderstanding:

“The game restarted, so everything before that is erased.”

That is simply incorrect.

Football governance clearly distinguishes between:

- The sporting outcome on the field, and

- The regulatory outcome after review

A referee allowing play to continue does not nullify a prior breach of the rules.

Disciplinary bodies exist precisely to address situations where infractions occurred, regardless of how the match was managed in real time.

Let me put it in a simple analogy:

Imagine a marathon runner who takes a shortcut off the official route, then re-enters the race and crosses the finish line first. At the moment, officials might not stop them. The race “finishes.”

But once the violation is reviewed, the result is corrected — because the rules of the competition were broken.

The same principle applies here.

Finally, claims of “disgrace,” “illegality,” or “backroom decisions” are speculative and not substantiated by the regulatory framework. These decisions are made within established procedures, and they remain subject to appeal — including at CAS.

So the bottom line is this:

Emotions about fairness are understandable.
But they do not override the rules.

And the rules are clear:
if a team leaves the field of play without authorization, it exposes itself to disciplinary sanctions — including forfeiture — even if the match was later resumed.

That’s not opinion.
That’s the regulatory framework.

Dima Maghrib ❤️🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦❤

author: @oulaich
text:
The truth about the AFCON 2025 final is simple: Football is governed by laws, not by who puts more pressure on the referee. When the Senegalese team decided to walk off the pitch for nearly 15 minutes in the 83rd minute, they didn’t just protest a call—they officially forfeited the match under CAF Regulations.
​According to Articles 82 and 84 of the tournament rules, any team that refuses to play or leaves the ground before the end of the match is considered the loser by a 3-0 scoreline. It is also important to recognize the impossible position the referee was put in. Faced with a complete walk-off and massive pressure from his superiors to avoid a total riot in the stands or further chaos on the field, he was forced to restart a game that legally should have ended right then and there.
​By forcing a restart on their own terms after such a long delay, Senegal disrupted the spirit of the final and ruined the celebration for millions. You cannot pause a continental final for 15 minutes and then expect the result to stand. Morocco stayed on the pitch, respected the referee, and followed the professional code of conduct. No matter what happened in extra time, the match was legally over the moment they walked toward the tunnel. Rules are rules, and in Rabat, the rule of law prevailed over the chaos.

Senegal did the same thing on November 12th 2016. It was the World Cup Qualifiers and Senegal were playing against South Africa. The ref awarded a "non-existent" penalty which gave them a 2-1 victory. Senegal protested later on, FIFA investigated and found the ref guilty for intentionally making false decisions to "make certain bets successful", he was eventually banned for life on March 2017 roughly 4 months after the match ended. The match was replayed on November 10th 2017, yes, an entire YEAR later, and ended 2-0 for Senegal, so they qualified to the World Cup.

      If y'all have no problem with a decision coming a year later, then neither should you have a problem with a decision coming 2 months later; either way, you're just shameless hypocrites.

author: @mightGalaxyBlackhole
text:
Embarrassing. Morocco should be ashamed of pursuing this title after they lost bitterly and justly to Senegal. 

The referee holds the ultimate authority and must be respected if you do not want to lose the match.

In this case, the match was restarted and eventually completed, despite the earlier disruption. Yes, the Senegalese team may have left the pitch at some point, but they returned and finished the game. That is what matters.

The referee is, in effect, the supreme authority on the field, the final arbiter. Once the match was concluded under his authority, that result should stand. CAF’s appeal committee should recognize this and refrain from overturning what was decided on the pitch. All the rules should consider that the match was completed on the pitch. 

Simply put, this trophy belongs to Senegal because the game was played to completion. We must avoid technicalities in our beautiful game.

author: @Slixis_3
text:
“IF A TEAM LEAVES THE FIELD” refers to the situation where the team, as a collective entity, leaves the field of play. In the present case, however, several players remained on the pitch. At most, this was only a partial departure by some players. Therefore, the automatic application of the article is not justified. It is a misreading of the text, arbitrary and unfair. THIS IS NOT LAW.
Had the referee definitively stopped the match, invoking Article 82 to award the championship title to one of the teams would have been legitimate. But the match continued. The victory was officially validated on paper. Law 5 of the International Football Association Board (IFAB), a fundamental principle of football law, states that the referee’s decisions regarding facts connected with play are final and irreversible. That simply settles the matter. Senegal retains the title. Any other decision would be contrary to morality.

author: @kisuke_1815
text:
To my dear European friends, have you forgotten the examples I will mention below that took place in your own continent, or do you not accept that African institutions can achieve justice? Do you expect Africa to remain associated with widespread mismanagement? It is time to move beyond this sense of superiority.

Football has already shown many times that matches and decisions don’t simply end with the final whistle—rules and justice can still be applied afterward, as seen in cases involving clubs like Inter Milan and Juventus. The 2006 Calciopoli scandal demonstrated that titles can be stripped and reassigned after proven violations, proving that outcomes can be corrected when wrongdoing is established.

A famous example is the 1991 match between AC Milan and Olympique de Marseille, where after a short interruption due to floodlights, Milan refused to resume play. As a result, Marseille was awarded the win and Milan faced sanctions, including exclusion from the competition.
This clearly shows that any team that refuses to continue or leaves the pitch must face the consequences—otherwise, it would open the door to chaos in football.

author: @LahlouAbde-f7j3n
text: The Senegalese national team displayed a blatant lack of respect towards the FIFA president, the CAF president, the tournament organizing committee, and the senior sports officials present at the stadium during the final match between Senegal and Morocco, the host country of the 2025 CAF World Cup in the Kingdom of Morocco. The players left the pitch for the locker room for approximately fifteen minutes. They may have showered, had a drink, or used their phones before returning to finish the match with utter insolence and disrespect. Their entry and exit from the pitch mid-match, without any legal basis, freely and without sanction, constitute the greatest disgrace in the history of world football. Furthermore, they provoked unrest and clashes between Senegalese supporters and Moroccan security forces, tarnishing what was arguably the best and most impressive tournament ever held in Africa. However, these actions were orchestrated and instigated by parties hostile to Morocco in order to sabotage Moroccan and continental celebrations. Therefore, severe sanctions are necessary, and the law must be enforced. This should serve as a lesson for the future of African and world football.

They walked off the pitch for 15 damn minutes, crying like victims, while two Moroccan security agents were killed and journalists had their equipment destroyed. Yet somehow, when Moroccan players are forced to deal with chaos and provocation, they’re painted as the villains.

This narrative is irrational and disgusting. From the first whistle, the world rushed to judge Morocco instead of looking at the facts. Senegal has already pulled similar scenes against Egypt and Tunisia — and now they do it again in an AFCON final, on the biggest stage, knowing exactly how the media will spin it.

author: @BanShieldSaving
text:
Howdy Mino&Amina, great job, amazing exposing, you did it :)

Here 31:00 we can see the one of the 7th gates of entrance (east side) to Tétwane old city wich was built in the mid-16th century, and its name dosn't means "okla=food" but the word written "okla door" we can spell it "3oqla door=bab L_3oqla" wich is derived from the word "3oqalâe=wise people".

After all, the name "3oqla door=bab L_3oqla" is linked to the "3oqalâe=wise people" who lived in the vicinity of this gate. That's all.

From TMP (TheMoroccanParisians) to (TheMoroccanKorians) 😅

Dear Content Publisher,



Several minutes ago. I was searching for content as part of my support work and research blog and I then found this video. It seemed amazing at first, but there's one Humongous issue.

Around this section of the video, 1:35, the word "Whiskey" is mentioned.

Regarding that. Morocco is a Muslim country and aligning that drink aswell as the word with the country is extremely offensive, since Islam teaches Muslims to stray away from the entirety of that drink aswell as similar drinks.

In line with that. As that part of your video is extremely offensive, would you thereby be able to resolve this serious issue please?

This issue needs to be resolved as soon as possible, as it can mentally affect Muslims in Morocco aswell as Muslims in general, since there are Muslims who take their faith very seriously and don't want to see their country aswell as other Muslim countries being aligned with the realm of alcoholism.

Hopefully you understand.

author: @sofianechenaoui4046
text:
Salut ! Je suis avec beaucoup de plaisir tes aventures à Marrakech et Agadir. En tant que Libanais vivant en France, c'est super de voir ton regard sur notre beau pays.
​Si tu en as l'occasion, il faut absolument que tu viennes faire un tour à Casablanca ! C'est le cœur battant du Maroc. Je serais ravi de t'accueillir et de te faire visiter la région pour te montrer une autre facette de l'ambiance marocaine : son énergie débordante, son mélange unique de modernité et de traditions, et surtout, la légendaire hospitalité casablancaise.
​On t'attend pour te faire découvrir la vraie vie de Casa et ses pépites cachées. À très bientôt j'espère !"

author: @user-oc8jp2bk2y
text:
I am not into this music genre, but why does this art feels like the essence of my childhood?

Huh, that was pretty intense
I barely listened to music, I just paused, started at the art, felt something and wrote that comment, and now I am editing it telling that apprently it helped me
This does convey the vibe of my childhood, and I had to reprocess it for a bit
My childhood mostly was not happy, and mostly, traumatic shit happened to happen at summers, so I spent most either gaming or walking with friends outside (most of them were not doing okay too)
So... I recalled some memories and had to cry for the first time about it, I would probably get to that later otherwise but it happened here

author: @SealEggs
text:
I originally found this song just trying to look for silly circus-y music and thought it sounded nice about a month ago, and the one night I look into the lyrics hit me so hard that it has me writing this monster.

I dont get emotional, ever. I've seen all the sad stories, all the cliches, and so many niches. I've written them myself for christ's sake. But this song nearly made me cry. Genuinely had to rub my eyes for a second. The story behind it is one of, if not THE only kind of "sad story" that genuinely hurts my soul: Amnesia in a love story. That trope alone kills me a little. Then paired with the music itself makes it unbelievably melancholy, yet so wonderous.

It's something of a masquerade. A beautiful and silly dance of strangers, like a serious circus, but with the masks all off, baring the depressing reality so raw. The girl's voice fits the song so stupidly well, and the emotional juxtaposition of the whimsical song and harrowing story made me feel like I was in the song. Like I was the man listening to her, suffering from all the love I have for a girl that just cant remember me. Amnesia's a somber thing in of itself, but when you pair it with love, it hits so much harder. Let alone music, which is emotion incarnate. Not to mention how good the words themselves are.

The lyrics are both written beautifully and sung  magnificently. They're so clearly conveyed, yet JUST opaque enough for me to jam to the song on it's own, and sad enough that when I hear them, my heart fumbles and drops in a pleasant way. The way she keeps reaffirming her worry, the way she asks to stop the tears, the way she's so confused the whole time, the constant references to her memory loss without ever growing repetitive, the way she's so sure being romantic with this "stranger" is right, and ESPECIALLY the way she reaffirms she'll love him again even after they're strangers again is so unfathomably beautiful.

I'm such an un-emotional person, usually so numb to emotional tragedies, and this song has somehow plucked every single super specific, super hidden string in my heart in such an otherworldly distinct, precise way that it has me writing this entire essay right now. Hell, I had to breathe in the middle of writing this for a sec, thinking over it again.

Beautiful fucking song. Perfect, dare I say. Fate is CRUEL for only showing this to me now.

You've earned my #1 favorite song of all time and rightfully so. And I've only ever had one song have that place: Asymmetry, by Reol. You've COMPLETELY knocked it off it's podium.
--
Nevermind. I cried.

author: @Amon-w7x
text:
I love this song, it's my favorite.

It helped me through difficult times in my life when I had many insecurities because my parents belittled me every day, even though I worked out, played guitar, studied various subjects, was polite, and had many other qualities that... I couldn't see anymore. In my mind, I was ugly, stupid, weak both psychologically and physically.

I was also very lonely after having toxic friends who literally almost broke my legs, and to make matters worse, I was in a relationship where the person I was dating controlled me and hurt me.

But this song helped me; it helped me to be more cheerful and optimistic. This song reminded me that I am not a burden and that one day I would find true love. Thank you, Tanger <3

author: @n1nsad
text:
Прошел год. Для меня этот трек остается одним из лучших что я слышал. Английский для меня не родной язык, но благодаря базовому его пониманию я немного уловил смысл текста при первом прослушивании, и мне очень понравилась история. Но когда я перевел текст и вникнул в него, я понял всю глубину. Я не могу не найти минусов у этого трека, он просто идеален. На меня еще сильно повлиял тот факт, что Tanger для меня всегда ассоциировался с очень подвижными треками, как наверное и многие я его узнал через BIKE. Визуал просто потрясающий, очень, прям очень подходит к музыке. 
Считаю strangers once again лучшим треком в альбоме prefer not to say. Спасибо Tanger за творчество, я очень благодарен за эту музыку!

А еще, мне бы хотелось чтобы у Вас появились официальные страницы артиста на русский стриминговых платформах, таких как VK Музыка или Яндекс Музыка, хочется слушать (не знаю как сказать, официально чтоли?) и это могло влиять на Вашу статистику. Еще раз большое спасибо за творчесвто!

author: @detaia-VwV-
text:
I have been listening to this on Tidal almost every day for a week straight. Its plays in my head as I'm doing stuff - brushing my teeth, eating my medication etc. The lyrics call to me soso much. The themes of forgetting something important, _someone_ important. Of letting go of your burdens and dancing freely, just for tonight. I can't express how deeply it strikes a chord in me - someone with memory confusion and anxiety who can only do things when she doesn't think about anything - no doubts or fears or winding thoughts that spiral out of control. And then there's the rhythm. The rhythm, the rhythm, the RHYTHM. Goodness. This has become one of the songs I listen to when I need to lose myself and get out of my own head. I really, really love this song. Thank you for makin this.
p.s  its impossible to pick my favourite part, but I especially like the bit which goes "my head, it hurts, it hurts, it hurts!". the way the singer delivers that line is sooo good. its cathartic to listen (or sing) to

author: @Radtechgirl
text:
For a country that talks about capitalism so much you would expect them to understand when you insult and threaten your customers they aren’t your customers any longer.

Thank you for pointing out that the worst insult has been calling us the “51st state”.  Your country threatens our sovereignty with this deep disrespect and are then shocked when we don’t spend our money in your country.  I have seen so many reports that people in the US think we aren’t coming because of your weather haven’t seen how many of us will go to Tim Horton in a full blizzard just for coffee and donuts.  Others blame the exchange rate which is not substantially different than it has been for a number of years.  We are now travelling to Europe and Asia for vacations and that is definitely not cheap.  We are making new habits and traditions instead of same old same old vacationing in a country that has shown us clearly that you are not our friends.  Your country just wants our money.

We have been deeply saddened to realize that it was only our people who thought we were great friends who look out for each other and stand up for each other when insulted or threatened by other countries.  

The North remembers.  We may not learn this lesson as quickly as we should but we do learn and we will not forget.

Canada has many friends and we are now growing much closer to them.  Middle powers are realizing our true worth.

Keep elbows up!  🇨🇦💪😠

author: @balthazarbeutelwolf9097
text: There is only a very small group of foreign tourists who are so political that the dislike of a particular government would deter them from travel. However, when there is a sense that those government's policies could directly impact their journey that changes drastically. Perhaps there is a non-neglegible risk that you are rejected at the border, forfeiting your hotel booking, never mind the transatlantic flight in vain. The erraticness of some of Trump's policies is also an issue: perhaps your visa-free status is revoked all of a sudden and you are supposed to fork out for an expenditure that was not on your bingo card. Producing your 5-year-history of social media was an idea floating in the air, seriously? What would that even entail for the permanently off-line? Besides politics, there were also stories of part of the US tourism industry fleecing world cup travellers, e.g. New Jersey train ticket prices going up 5-fold.

author: @tigervalley62
text:
I'm a local White English South African born post Apartheid. Never left the country, not even to neighbouring countries like Zimbabwe, Lesotho, and Botswana. Grew up this way basically my entire life. Heard from many people that our "gated fortresses" as you call them are not normal. I can understand that if you grew up in a society where you could just walk to the nearest store to get a packet of chips. But if you were born and raised in that environment, it is normal for someone like me. If that makes sense. If it's all you've ever known, that's normal. If a bird doesn't recognise he is in a cage, then is he really jailed mentally speaking? 

Outsiders can be shocked and horrified by my words on this situation, but it is a process of renormalisation of the post-apartheid South Africa. Living in gated communities and fortresses is how we do things around here. Would I like it to change? Who wouldn't? Nobody sane likes crime, but like I said, if it's all you've known, with absolutely 0 prospects of it changing even if the ANC lose power, it becomes normal. You learn to live with the problems and come to accept the reality as you get used to it.

I would go so far as to say, unfortunately crime is one of the smallest problems we have in this country as crazy as that might sound. We have bigger fish to fry before we tackle crime, if we tackle it at all. I doubt the crime will be solved in South Africa during my lifetime.

author: @keithosmond5730
text:
Re the new Americans (and those from other countries as well) who now have a way to gain Canadian citizenship...
In the past, if a male Canadian moved to another country, and did not renounce his Canadian citizenship, his descendants had a right to claim Canadian citizenship.
But if a woman moved to another country and married someone from there, she automatically lost her citizenship and the right of her descendants to apply for Canadian citizenship based on ancestry.
This breached the Charter's section on discrimination.
A few years ago, the SCC ruled that this was wrong, and the government needed to find a way to give those illegally cut off a path to reclaim that right. It took a while, but in December the Act allowing this was proclaimed.
Anyone who believes in equality between the sexes should have no issue with this.
Just because they now have a path doesn't mean they will take advantage of the option. I expect the vast majority will not. We haven't been flooded by requests by descendants of male Canadians. There will undoubtedly be an uptick, but nothing unmanageable.

author: @DragonToothlessTwitch
text:
As a Tri-citizen living in Japan (Canada by birth (Hometown: Ottawa), US by naturalization, and British by descent), I only use passports from Canada and UK. While the Canadian passport has an extra burden regarding the passport photo certification (which is a major pain in Japan as most photographers don't have a stamp to apply to the back of the photo), it still beats the feeling of being associated with the way the US has gone way to the fascism right-side of the political spectrum (social media having helped keep Americans in a smaller and smaller bubble of false reality).

I do share things about my adult years in the US before moving to Japan, but my students only care about learning of my life in Canada growing up (I moved to the US when I was 18 due to my dad's work). They like learning about my black bear encounters, tapping maple trees for sap which would become maple syrup, seeing Ottawa's Winterlude which is similar to the local Sapporo Snow Festival, and they like that unlike the American ALTs they've had in the past (who just want to go do their own thing at the end of the day and not interact with students), I'm willing to spend time helping students clean the classrooms. Many of those students have expressed interest in visiting Canada instead of the US, or even doing semesters in Canadian universities and thus avoiding the US altogether, because of my civility and genuine respect.

author: @Asmarman3
text:
L'objectif n'est pas de se qualifier pour la Coupe du monde, mais de concourir pour le titre et de battre les grandes équipes. Les équipes arabes et africains sont loin de cela, à l'exception du Maroc, toujours premier dans tous les disciplines notamment de l' athlétisme du Aouita, et motawakil en 84 passant par le hicham el garouje salah hissou, bakali...la liste est longue.
En foot, le premier point pour les Arabes et l' Afrique dès leur première participation en 1970. En 1986, ils passent le premier tour à se qualifier en tant que leader du  groupe relevé. Enfin, ils sont les premiers à se qualifier pour les demi-finales de la Coupe du monde au Qatar en 2022. 
Aucune équipe arabe et africaine qui ne leur ressemble aujourd'hui, surtout depuis 2018. Le Maroc est le visage du football africain et arabe, non seulement parmi les grandes équipes, mais dans toutes les catégories : 
champion d'Afrique U17 à deux reprises et demi-finaliste de la dernière Coupe du monde en 2023. 
Champion d'Afrique U20, est désormais champion du monde en 2025 au Chili.  
champion d'Afrique U23, et médaillés de bronze olympiques en 2024 à Paris, 
l'équipe locale, triple championne d'Afrique d' affilié (2018, 2020 et 2024). 
Et enfin, l'équipe senior, quatrième au monde en demi-finale en 2022.
 Sans oublier les équipes féminines et de futsal, où le Maroc domine l'Afrique et le monde arabe avec sept coupes consécutives… Le Maroc est le seul pays à avoir d'avantages des talons dans tous les championnats continentaux et mondiaux…

Les deux seules vrais nation en Afrique sont l'Éthiopie et le Maroc toute les autres nations ne sont que des inventions coloniale 😂.
Le Marocain a dans sont ADN l'amour du pays que tu ne retrouve pas chez les pays d'inventions moderne c'est pas plus compliqué.

Le Maroc est un Empire, comme l'Éthiopie et ont pris différents nom au travers les siècles.
Ce qu'on appelait les Maures venant de la diminution de Mauritanie ce n'est que le Maroc actuel l'Andalousie n'est que l'extension du Maroc. L'Éthiopie s'appeler à un époque l'empire d'Abyssinie.

C'est pourquoi la plupart de leurs joueurs sont nés dans des villes européennes et n'ont jamais vécu au Maroc, mais on ne peut pas leur enlever leur identité marocaine, contrairement aux Africains noirs qui choisissent tous de jouer pour l'Angleterre, l'Allemagne, la France, etc.
Le Maroc montre que si les Africains peuvent représenter l'Afrique, ils ont une chance de remporter une Coupe du monde à l'avenir.

author: @BZ3192
text: This reminds me of CS Lewis talking about the tin soldier in Mere Christianity and how God  wants to turn him into a real, living man. At first, the soldier might be content just being cleaned up or slightly improved (emotional stability, being more acceptable in society, developing better habits, etc)—but the transformation God intends is far more radical and therefore frightening. It’s not about making the tin soldier better at being tin… it’s about changing its very nature. That’s what’s frightening to us all who are looking for a “new story.”

To me it looks like Theo is having the same problem as Jordan Peterson has with Christianity.

They both treat it with a very high level of respect because they're both dealing with the core meanings of what Christianity is saying.

They both seem to know that it is an actual tussel with God himself that will leave you shattered.

I think we frequently do a disservice to the Gospel because we pretend like those struggles for the core of what man is aren't real.

Instead we treat Christianity as behavior modification when behavior and Christ clearly aren't on the same level because behavior can be corrupt and Christ can't be. (So they're clearly not the same thing.)

author: @Waveluth
text:
Theo, I’m an old man that has been through Hell on earth and when I tell you I KNOW YOUR SOUL. My life has been a mess in the past and somehow I am still here. I’ve buried my children, I feel so alone all the time even though I’m surrounded by people. I am also a broken man. I have begged The Lord to enter me and do whatever he sees fit to do with my worthless existence. I know Theo, listen your a good boy, I’ve been a fan of yours for many years. I’ve seen the sadness and your  empty heart. I’ve asked, prayed, begged, I’ve given away my money to strangers. 
Now I live day to day. I have a disabled 42 yr old son, and a15 yr old with Autism. I care for them, I am their world but I’ve begged God to step in to my life so I can take better care of myself. I skip Medicine I need to buy food. I e never taken charity in my life and I never will. But I sure would like to feel peace and happiness. 
You see son, even facing my life as it is. I still keep going at life. 
You have made a life that most folks would love to have. You have money ( I presume) you have shelter and food when your hungry and go pick up medicine if you have too. 
Be happy with your story. Don’t change the story just enhance it with acts of kindness and ask the Lord to watch over you as you navigate your life. I’m so sad to see you cry. You have so much Theo, remember those that do not. I’m broke always but I still help people when I can. Mostly strangers. 
Try changing your life by doing one positive thing each day.  
I’m hoping God will hear my prayers for you.  I’ll say a prayer for you everyday I promise you. Things will change for the better, trust me. 
Theo please talk to your friends or someone if you start having feelings that you know are wrong. Take care buddy ok. 
Bless you 
From an old man that sees your pain. Trust in the Lord.  
✌️❤️🙏😎🔱

author: @Red_Eyed
text:
Crazy, but weed helped me get closer to the lord. Whenever I was high, I would start having therapy sessions with myself and God. I would ask and He would show me. I’m not saying I need the plant like I need Him, but it would always get me in the perfect state of mind to work on myself. But it was a double edged sword. Every time I smoke, it’s like I’m being attacked. Negative memories would flood my mind and I would have to combat them. I’d pray whatever is attacking me to be gone and it would. That’s how I would deep dive into myself and start fixing things with the Lord. 
I’m about a week off smoking and I don’t miss it. Crazy cuz I’ve been smoking almost religiously since I turned 18. I’m 36 and feeling great now. Hahah. God is good, y’all. Atheist for 30 years then I prayed just like Theo. Asking for my next chapter and asking to teach me love. He sent me my 2 cats and I’m actually in my second chapter now. He answered my prayers even after I turned my back on Him. Ask Him with an open heart and He answers. I promise. 🙏

It’s a great thing that you have your eyes on God and you want to change. But it’s vital that you know who God is. Jesus is the only way to the Father, you are separated right now and without Jesus you can’t know God. Also, it’s my experience that God wants you so surrender everything at the door. The reason Theo won’t open the door when Jesus is banging on it loud and clear is that he wants to bring some things with him like some preconceived notions, some baggage and God won’t accept anything but a full surrender. Some people get to an age where their pride won’t let them come to their father like a little child, and if Theo knew that Jesus is the God of the Old Testament and his best and only way to salvation from the things that are hurting him, then he will think God is just another word in a prayer or an idea you debate about. He is probably thinking about “everything I have to give up” when in reality, God will free him from the poison weighing him down and let him be who he was always meant to be. Pray for the man, in Jesus name.

author: @nacerchabni7469
text:
الكل يريد مصلحته ...المروك يعتمد على امريكا ترمب .ان يضغط على الجزاءر . كما يطلب من فرنسا ..كذلك.
والجزاءر ليست المروك المنبطح المطبع المطيع.
هي سيدة مواقفها ولا احد يملي عليها..
الجزاءر شعب وحكومة وجيش ومؤسسات ترفض فتح الحدود وخاصة في هذه الضروف.
الصحراء الغربية لاهلها وشعبها وجيشها وليست ملك لفرنسا او المروك او ترامب. ولا احد منهم يملك عقد ملكية الصحراء الغربية حتى يتصرف فيها كمايشاء.
المالك والممثل الشرعي والوحيد لارضه هو الشعب الصحراوي . كل الاحكام العدل الدولية والتاريخية اثبتت بان لا علاقة المروك ولا سيادة على الصحراء الغربية.

author: @HonaWatan
text:
Clarification to the Sports Public Opinion Regarding the Organization of AFCON 2025 in Morocco
The organization of the Africa Cup of Nations 2025 in Morocco generated extensive debate within African sports and media circles, often moving beyond objective sporting assessment into media distortion and political interpretation. Responsible discussion requires placing facts in their proper context.
1. The Context of Morocco’s Success
Morocco is not a sudden phenomenon in African football. It became:
The first African nation to reach the World Cup semifinals (Qatar 2022), finishing fourth globally.
This achievement elevated African football as a whole, contributing to increased respect and representation on the world stage.
Morocco has invested for years in world-class sports infrastructure, including stadiums, training centers, and logistics systems.
Such progress naturally generated resistance from parties uncomfortable with shifting leadership dynamics in African football.
2. Organization and Hospitality: Established Facts
All participating teams were accommodated in hotels of at least five-star standards.
Dedicated and fully equipped training facilities were provided for each team.
Transportation, security, medical services, and logistics met CAF requirements.
These are verifiable facts, not subjective claims.
3. Moroccan Fans and Sporting Conduct
Moroccan supporters:
Welcomed and supported friendly and brotherly teams, notably Senegal and Egypt.
Actively supported Senegal throughout its journey to the final.
Were not involved in organized violence or unsportsmanlike behavior during the tournament.
Conversely, certain accusations emerged only after defeats, without factual substantiation.
4. Refereeing and the Final Match
Historically, Moroccan teams have faced controversial refereeing decisions internationally without resorting to disorder.
During the final:
A refereeing incident escalated into on-field chaos.
Media narratives shifted the issue from sport to political hostility.
Campaigns emerged aiming to damage Moroccan–Senegalese relations.
More concerning was the violence against Moroccan residents in Senegal and the destruction of their property—events that cannot be justified by sport.
5. Between Sport and Politics
The final was exploited to:
Undermine Morocco’s image as a host nation.
Create division between two historically allied peoples.
Politicize a sporting event by linking it to unrelated regional issues.
6. Morocco’s Position
Morocco chose de-escalation, prioritizing stability, public safety, and sportsmanship over confrontation. Senegal won the trophy; Morocco preserved ethical responsibility.
Conclusion
Morocco won the organization. Africa won a successful tournament.

author: @لحاجلحاج-ب3ج
text: بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم اعوذ بالله من الشيطان الرجيم بسم الله الذي لا يضر مع اسمه شيء في الارض ولا في السماء وهو السميع العليم قل للذين كفرو ستغلبون ستغلبون وتحشرون الى جهنم وبئس المصير  وادوك المنافقين لي خدامين مع إسرائيل جميعا واش محشمتوش المجرمين بعتو راسكم او بعتو دين ديالكوم اليهود وادوك العيالات لي زعما موظفات عند الصهاينة او كيحسبو راسهم مسلمات فراسكم صافي راكم تمسختو او تبهدلتو  واش المنافقين ماشي حشومة عليكم تحسبو راسكم مسلمين او كديرو الخدمة ديال اليهود هادوك المجرمين لي كاينين فالمحاكم جميعا اش كيديرو كيعتاقلو فالمغاربة هادوك المجرمين لي سماوهوم جندارم او البوليس اش كيديرو را كيديرو غير الخدمة ديال اليهود اشمن وراق كيطلبو هاد الإجرام كامل لي كديرو فالمغاربة واش كاين فشي آية قرآنية حاضيين غير ديرو ليهم روشيرش اوعتاقلوهوم او سرقوهوم را الخدمة ديال الإستعمار من بكري را هيا هادي سير نعلة الله وعليكم الى يوم دين وعلى الكافرين والمنافقين جميعا المجرمين ولا تجسس رديتوها  مخابارات  را المسلمين فرض عليهم الله غير الاركان ديال الإسلام او راه لا يمكن واحد بايع دينو او معندو لا جد لا تاسيلا او فنفس الوقت كيدير الخدمة ديال اليهود او قال ليك كيعرف الحق والقانون هاد الخدمة لي كتوقع فالطرقان او بعد الإدارات را مكيديروها ايلا المجرمين او متيحاربش المسلمين ايلا اليهود صافي المجرمين اسقاط نظام صهيوني واسقاط الملكية مابقات لا الإنتخابات لا والو صافي جاء الحق وزهق الباطل ان الباطل كان زهوقا  اقسم بالله بإذن الله وقوة الله ايلا انا هو لي غنكون سباب ديالكوم فالخراب هاهوا كلشي بان ليكم من المجرم ديال محمد السادس باش نوريه استهزء بالله ورسوله لانه صافي را وصلتو البلافو فتاحيماريت المغاربة معذبين فالسوجون او كتحسبوهوم بالإرهاب راه الله لي خلق هاد دنيا ماشي دوك الصهاينة لي كيعطيوكوم تعليمات والله لا بقيتو فيها بإنسكم وجنكم راه الله لي غادي ايدير كلشي انا غنكون غير السبب راه انا قال لينا الله لا تزكو انفسكم را كنهضر مع الحق سبحانه كنتكل  على الحي الذي لا يموت ماشي كنتسنا المجرم ديال فرنسا اعطيني تعليمات ديال الفحشاء والمنكر لي كيسجن المغاربة او كيريب ليهم ديورهوم او كيحسبهوم بالحيوان  احسبوه الصهاينة مسؤول كبير  حورما سيدي ربي نزل جنودك احكم بيننا وبين هاد القوم الظالمين بالحق ولا حول ولا قوة الا بالله العلي العضيم

author: @user-f2hjytop
text:
مراكش هي تسمية امازيغية سماها المرابطين كعاصمة لهم بما انهم مسلمين سماو عاصمتهم  ارض الله بالخلفية الدينية 
مور = بالأمازيغية الارض 
اكوش = بالأمازيغية الله الخالق 
وتجمع مراكش او اموراكش بالأمازيغية 
وتوجد قرية صغيرة في شرق المغرب بين جبال الاطلس تسما تمراكشت تصغير وتدليل لتلك القرية تيمنا بمراكش المدينة الكبير مراكش
وللمعلومة مور هي نفسها موروكو اي ارض شعب المور بهدا نحن نقول اننا لسنا عرب نحن شعب المور وسكان موروكو هم موريون امازيغ
Morokko
Morocco
Maroc
Marueccos 
جميع شعوب الارض عبر عصور يعلمون ان سكان هده الارض  ومعروف لديهم ان سكان موروكو هم المور .
القوم والوحيدين اللدين  حروفو تسميته مور هما العرب من الخليج ومعروف عن العرب يغيرون تسمية اي قوم لقتل تاريخهم وطمسه مثل تينيس صارت تونس المعنى لهاد بل تينيس هي المفتاح اي مفتاح افريقيا .
اخر انجازات العرب تسمية منتخب جزر الكومر الى جزر القمر 🌙 وصراحة لاعلاقة  😂😂😂😂😂😂 العرب محترفين في تزوير وجب عدم الاخد من مصادرهم في التاريخ
ونزيدكم ترجمة 
المملكة المغربيةادا ترجمت  بالانجليزية هي westernocco 
وليس Morocco 
اما ادا قمنا لترجمة Morocco الى العربية فسنحصل على 
مملكة المور اي شعب المور 
بهده تسمية مغرب هو اتجاه وليس اسم بلد وشعبه لان مملكة المور تنفي العروبة عنها ادا لاتصلح لهم بما انهم يريدون تعريبها 😂😂😂😂😂😂😂😂 الحمد لله على نعمة البصيرة

author: @FatimaBatoul-y9c
text:
السلام عليكم و رحمة اللّٰه وبركاته 🤍 لا يجوز نشر صور نساء متبرجات فالتبرج هو إظهار المرأة لما يجب عليها ستره من الزينة أو الجسد أمام الرجال الأجانب، سواء كان ذلك في اللباس أو المشي أو التصرفات.  قال اللّٰه عزَّ و جلَّ بعد أعوذ باللّٰه من الشيطان الرجيم﷽:«وَقُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنَاتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصَارِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَّ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلَى جُيُوبِهِنَّ (٣١) النّور  قال اللّٰه عزَّ وجلَّ بعد أعوذ باللّٰه من الشيطان الرجيم ﷽ :« وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ تَبَرُّجَ الْجَاهِلِيَّةِ الْأُولَى (٣٣)  الأحزاب وقال «اللّٰه» عز وجل بعد أعوذ باللّٰه من الشيطان الرجيم بسم اللّٰه الرحمن الرحيم:«يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُل لِّأَزْوَاجِكَ وَبَنَاتِكَ وَنِسَاءِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ يُدْنِينَ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِن جَلَابِيبِهِنَّ ذَلِكَ أَدْنَى أَن يُعْرَفْنَ فَلَا يُؤْذَيْنَ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا(٥٩)  الأحزاب   قال رسول اللّٰهﷺ:«- صِنْفَانِ من أهلِ النارِ لمْ أَرَهُما بَعْدُ : قومٌ مَعَهُمْ سِياطٌ كَأَذْنابِ البَقَرِ يَضْرِبُونَ الناسَ بِها ، ونِساءٌ كَاسِياتٌ عَارِياتٌ ، مُمِيلاتٌ مائِلاتٌ ، رُؤوسُهُنَّ كَأَسْنِمَةِ البُخْتِ المائِلَةِ ، لا يَدْخُلَنَّ الجنةَ ، ولا يَجِدَنَّ رِيحَها ، وإِنَّ رِيحَها لَيوجَدُ من مَسِيرَةِ كذا وكذا
خلاصة حكم المحدث : صحيح
الراوي : أبو هريرة | المحدث : الألباني | المصدر : صحيح الجامع | الصفحة أو الرقم : 3799
| التخريج : أخرجه مسلم (2128) باختلاف يسير
قال «اللّٰه» عزَّ وجلَّ بعد أعوذ باللّٰه من الشيطان الرجيم﷽:«...وَتَحْسَبُونَهُ هَيِّنًا وَهُوَ عِندَ اللَّهِ عَظِيم

أرجو أن تتفهمي أني ما قصدت أبدًا الإساءة أو جرحك، وإنما أردت أن أذكّر نفسي وأذكّرك بما شرعه «اللّٰه» عز وجل من سترٍ وحجابٍ صيانةً للمرأة ورفعةً لمقامها.
لفظ التبرج في الشرع معناه إظهار ما أمر «اللّٰه» بستره أمام الأجانب، وليس معناه الفاحشة ولا الزنا أبدًا.
بل على العكس، أنتِ أخت عزيزة، وأتمنى لكِ كل خير وتوفيق، وإنما نصيحتي من باب المحبة في «اللّٰه»؛ لأننا سنُسأل جميعًا بين يديه سبحانه عمّا عرضنا ونشرنا.
أسأل «اللّٰه» أن يشرح صدرك للحق ويزينك بزينة الستر والعفاف، فهي أجمل ما تتزين به المرأة ، ولا شيء يستحق أن نخسر الجنة لأجله 🌸 
قال «اللّٰه» عزَّ وجلَّ بعد أعوذ باللّٰه من الشيطان الرجيم﷽:« وَتَعَاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوَى وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقَابِ(٢)  المائدة أي لا يجوز لك أن تساعد غيرك على معصية أو باطل، سواء بالفعل أو القول أو حتى بالدعاية له.   قال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم:"من دعا إلى هدى كان له من الأجر مثل أجور من تبعه، لا ينقص ذلك من أجورهم شيئًا، ومن دعا إلى ضلالة كان عليه من الإثم مثل آثام من تبعه، لا ينقص ذلك من آثامهم شيئًا." (رواه مسلم) قال «اللّٰه» عزَّ وجلَّ بعد أعوذ باللّٰه من الشيطان الرجيم﷽:«...وَتَحْسَبُونَهُ هَيِّنًا وَهُوَ عِندَ اللَّهِ عَظِيمٌ(١٥)  النّور و في الأشهر الحرم( رجب، ذو القعدة،ذو الحجة، محرم) الذنب مضاعف فيها وكذلك الأجر ،قال «اللّٰه» عزَّ وجلَّ بعد أعوذ باللّٰه من الشيطان الرجيم:«إِنَّ عِدَّةَ الشُّهُورِ عِندَ اللَّهِ اثْنَا عَشَرَ شَهْرًا فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ يَوْمَ خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ مِنْهَا أَرْبَعَةٌ حُرُمٌ ذَلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ فَلَا تَظْلِمُوا فِيهِنَّ أَنفُسَكُمْ.....(٣٦)  التوبة ونشر المحظورات يعتبر مجاهرة بالمعصية قال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم:«كل أمتي معافى إلا المجاهرين...»رواه البخاري (6069) ومسلم (2990)
